focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 7

Earlier that Saturday, while Tiana was still out and the apartment still believed in next week, Marcia had spent the money so many times in her head it already felt warm from handling. First and security on a place with a real front door, not a converted garage somebody else could decide to call illegal whenever it suited them. The college money she had not named to Tiana because hope got loud in that apartment, and loud hope turned into appetite. She would clear the light bill and the little late fees that bred in the dark when you let them. The numbers changed depending on which office wanted blood first, but the total never moved: five thousand eight hundred dollars, enough to turn July from a cliff into chores.

Inside her handbag she carried three white envelopes with her block printing in the corners: RENT, SCHOOL, LIGHT. She had folded them flat before leaving, the same way she folded medication schedules for Celia Alvarez, crisp enough that even bad news had to stand up straight. Writing the labels ahead of time felt half like discipline and half like temptation. She had done it anyway. A woman could not wait on miracle and then pretend she had no use for it once it came.

People with checking accounts liked to shelve partner among quaint old-country habits, beside rum cake and airport barrels. Marcia never corrected them. Let them keep their little smile. A hand was not folklore. A hand was what people built when the bank charged thirty-five dollars because a paycheck landed one day wrong, when credit asked for paper the poor could not spare, when cash disappeared too easily inside a marriage or a family or your own hungry head. Twenty-nine names, a hundred dollars every week, and Marcia had carried two numbers because one number was grocery hope....
prev_tail_excerpt:
...d cigarettes he could smoke ambition. She could stretch one chicken over two nights and walk one way to church if the transit card ran out. She had done uglier things for less.

Humiliation from Elena's kitchen had already hardened into assignments. Tomorrow she would give Yvette the full amount due before Delroy could start talking around it. Tonight she would strip the borrowed bus money back out of the grocery money and call the light company for one more week. If Elena cut tomorrow short, fine. If Delroy brought in cash this week, part of it would go straight into the next envelope before rice, before phone, before any bill that could be delayed without a man appearing at the door.

She would not tell the children how close the math sat to breaking yet. Fear made young people stupid in different directions. Tiana got colder. Micah got louder. Neither one of them needed to see how much of the summer was balanced on women passing envelopes after church and pretending that trust was stronger than misfortune.

Little River announced itself before the turn: salt, old fryer grease, damp cotton, somebody's cheap coconut lotion giving up under the heat. The cold air had gone thin and useless. The partner notebook stayed in her hand for the rest of the ride. She did not grip it. She held it. She ran her thumb over the cardboard cover until the edge softened with sweat. She slipped the notebook back into her bag as carefully as if it were breakable and gave the rest of the summer its rule. Part of it was already spoken for in her head: whatever Tiana needed to carry north without looking hungry or lost. July was the door she had: thin wood with a cheap lock, maybe swollen from rain already. Until it failed, every other bill in the house would have to wait its turn outside it.
